sources.name: SWE-bench Verified
sources.representativeness: Human-validated subset of real GitHub issues. Good control benchmark because many tasks are short compared with TaskOps target work.
sources.reproducibility_note: Useful baseline, but less diagnostic for long-running orchestration.

sources.name: SWE-bench Pro
sources.representativeness: Long-horizon codebase issue repair with Docker-based evaluation. Strong core benchmark for TaskOps paper experiments.
sources.reproducibility_note: Qwen reports a corrected/refined public set and an internal bash/file-edit scaffold. Direct reproduction should pin exact task filtering and harness.

sources.name: SWE-bench Multilingual
sources.representativeness: Useful extension to show TaskOps behavior beyond Python-heavy SWE-bench Verified.
sources.reproducibility_note: Keep as extension until public harness and task set are pinned.

sources.name: Terminal-Bench 2.0
sources.representativeness: Real terminal tasks spanning build, security, systems, and debugging workflows. High value for TaskOps queue/retry/evidence tests.
sources.reproducibility_note: Qwen reports Harbor/Terminus-2 harness, 3h timeout, 32 CPU/48 GB RAM, and average of 5 runs.

sources.name: SkillsBench
sources.representativeness: Measures whether agents can use modular skills and procedural instructions. Directly relevant to TaskOps as a protocol/skill-mediated system.
sources.reproducibility_note: Qwen reports OpenCode on 78 self-contained tasks, excluding API-dependent tasks, average of 5 runs.

sources.name: NL2RepoBench
sources.representativeness: 0-to-1 repository generation from natural language. Good for decomposition, architecture, packaging, and multi-file implementation.
sources.reproducibility_note: Qwen notes others are evaluated via Claude Code with temp=1.0, top_p=0.95, max_turns=900; exact Qwen harness should be pinned before claiming reproduction.

sources.name: HCAST public subset
sources.representativeness: Human-calibrated autonomous software tasks with time-horizon framing. Best fit for paper claims about 1h to 8h+ work.
sources.reproducibility_note: Full suite is not fully public. Public subset is still valuable for TaskOps time-horizon experiments.

sources.name: RE-Bench
sources.representativeness: Seven open-ended ML research engineering environments with 8-hour human expert attempts. Strong 8h-class benchmark.
sources.reproducibility_note: Compute-heavy and task count is small. Use as high-value long-work slice rather than broad suite.

sources.name: Agents' Last Exam
sources.representativeness: Broad, economically valuable real-work tasks with hidden-reference evaluation. Use as external oracle for TaskOps graph-to-artifact work.
sources.reproducibility_note: Infra-heavy: VM images, GCP setup, Windows/licensed/GPU tasks. Start with selected Linux deterministic tasks.

sources.name: TheAgentCompany
sources.representativeness: Simulated software-company workplace tasks spanning web, code, programs, and communication. Good extension for multi-app TaskOps coordination.
sources.reproducibility_note: More operationally complex than core coding benchmarks; keep as extension until TaskOps adapter matures.

sources.name: Vending-Bench
sources.representativeness: Long-term coherence benchmark where individually simple decisions compound over a long simulated horizon.
sources.reproducibility_note: Useful for ultra-long coherence narrative, but less directly tied to code/task execution.

sources.name: Claw-Eval
sources.representativeness: Highly relevant to OpenClaw-native work but not suitable as core paper evidence unless the harness is published or pinned.
sources.reproducibility_note: Use as contextual motivation, not as a core reproducible benchmark.

sources.name: QwenClawBench
sources.representativeness: Potentially close to real OpenClaw usage distribution, but currently not public enough for paper-core reproduction.
sources.reproducibility_note: Use as motivation only unless benchmark definition becomes public.
